speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of the interview with Nathan Peters, June 9th. When we were finishing up the last tape, you were talking about leaving the home where you had been placed with a family in Montreal, and setting off on your own with a friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. With a friend and went to work. I went-- I went into the men's suit business. I mean, to-- to work as a-- I worked as a cutter. And when-- this was some-- sometimes I-- I-- I believe, in '48, already. And-- and then in '49, my family came to the United-- to-- to the United States, in November of '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get permission to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they were brought out by the-- by the American Joint. And somebody signed-- signed the papers for them, and they came out. My next goal was to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the time that you were in Montreal, were you able to write to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I wrote to them. I sent packages to them. We-- it was just-- it was just not official. I mean, as far as everybody knew, I had-- I had family, you know, my mother. And we-- so we corresponded all the time. And when they came out in '49-- they came out around Christmas of '49, just-- just before the end of the year. And then I put through a search to find them. Because they are-- because to emigrate from Canada over here, at-- at that time-- even today, I believe-- at that time it was almost impossible unless you marry somebody over here, or it's a son or a daughter or-- or a parent. I couldn't even come for a visit. They could come to visit me. But I could not come to visit them because I wasn't a citizen yet. And I didn't-- So I put a search through, to find them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that-- what do you mean, you put out a search?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put in the paper that I'm looking for this and that, those and-- people, and describe them and all this stuff. And then-- then-- then I found them, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how you found them? Through the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's not really-- I didn't find them through a paper, but I put it up to find-- to-- to get legally, to be able to come to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was part of the legal game--

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the legal game to play.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you had to play?

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you knew all the time, where they were, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where did they go? Where did they come when they came--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to New York. They lived in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had one sister, but she was really not in any financial-- to be able to help. I mean, she was a seamstress, and you know, she used take care of herself. Her husband was not alive no more. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who came? This was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my brother, my sister, and my aunt. They all came. Yes, they all-- all-- all came here. And they-- they lived in the Bronx. So I put through a search. And obviously, I found them. And, I mean, I got all these papers in order. It took me 10 days. And I came here July 4th of 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, once I came there, we went to the consul. And I showed them I have a parent in the-- over here. And so it was, like, 10, 12 days, I have my visa, and I came over here. So I came here in 1950, and then I couldn't get a job because I was draft age.

speaker: interviewee
text: (WHISPERS) The Korean--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Korean War. And everybody tells you crazy-- you should not go to the United States right now because of the Korean War. And 1953, I went to work. I went to work for a guy. I-- really from the bottom up. Because I was really sweeping floors and-- and doing some-- some cutting. But I really didn't-- about 1950--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was this in New York City, in the Garment District?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City, in the Garment Center. Well, in 1953, I got myself up where I was making a nice living with the same company. And then I was drafted to the Army. 19-- 1953, May 17, I went into the Army. I took my basic training in Camp Gordon, Georgia. I want to Signal School. And then they sent me back to Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn your English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was fortunate, again, when they set me up in Canada, with a Jewish family, you know, they couldn't speak anything, no Yiddish. So I had no choice. So I-- besides going to-- to school, at night school, I had no choice but to speak English with them. So those few months I was with them was very helpful. And then I really then was in the Army, obviously, for-- I was in the army for almost two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they station you in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Nuremberg and Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time we [? act ?] [? what-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it just-- to me, it wasn't-- it was just like a waste of time. Because I was never going to stay in the Army. And I was already married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I got married [? the ?] of May-- May in 1953. And I went into the army August 17, also. Three months to the date. So I really was just to put in time, but you know, soon they offered some early discharge with that, too. That's why I was only 21 months instead of-- instead of-- instead of two years. Then when I came back, Korea, my daughter was born while I was away. OK. Let's pull back a little bit. Tell me how you met Shirley.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met-- I met Shirley in-- on a trip to Washington. We went three couples? Let's see. We went, three couples, to Washington. And she was somebody else's date. And I was somebody else's date. And it's really a very long-- [LAUGHTER] But at that time, going to Washington was a long trip. It's not like today, where it is in '95. You know, we had the-- and-- well, we try--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you drive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a car and I drove. The deal was that I wasn't going to drive because I worked every Saturday. But the guy who's supposed to drive my car drove it. But the time-- he almost got us killed. So I figure I better drive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Washington. We drove almost all night. And that's how I met Shirley. And I'm sure she's not going to like that. But she probably pulled the oldest trick. She left-- she left something in my glove compartment. And she-- and she called me the following day. She said, it's there? So-- So you had to get together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the three guys together and the three girls together? Is that how it went?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we are all coupled up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you were all couples?

speaker: interviewee
text: All coupled up with each other, yeah. And that's how I met Shirley, on a trip to Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you each had to disentangle from the other person? The other couple? Part-- part of the couple.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, she and I drove-- drove. So we were so tired. We went to sleep in the car for-- for five hours. Because we really-- then we-- then we were-- we toured Washington. And we started-- that was in 1952. And after that, we started dating. And we got engaged by the end of 1952. We got married in May 17, 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were Shirley born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Brooklyn, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her parents? Where did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They come from Hungary. But they-- they came here before the war. They were not-- they lost a lot of family. But her father and mother came here be-- before the war, came after the Second World War, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the first one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, after the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, so then what was the date that you got married? Say that again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: In August 17, 1953, I was in the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was already expecting--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Later. She wanted to go to Europe with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1954, if you remember, the Indochina War, when the-- when the French pulled out and the Americans came in? And being during a war in Europe, I said, no way you're going to Europe with me. So the Korean War ended. So she want to become pregnant. It was not an accident.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- and she stayed back here. Says you can't go to Europe. Because we don't know if-- because if they-- if the American go in, that was '54. We're probably going-- the European are probably the first one to be sent there. But then, you know, they-- they had that phony truce, the whole deal. There's nothing-- nothing really happened till later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in-- in Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your daughter was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did come home and-- for Christmas, to see her. I mean, if I had furlough. And I came home to see her in Christmas of '54. And then I went back to-- I came home May of 1955. I went back to my old job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the company you were working for? Or the man you were working for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's TJ [? Borelli. ?] And I worked there. I came back, got my-- my job back. And I stayed there till 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went into business. We-- we-- It was myself and a-- another person, American-born again. And we wanted to buy part of his business. But he refused. He didn't want to do it. It was his own reason. And we left and they went into business. We stayed in business-- actually, we stayed together for 35 years, I mean, till we-- he retired a year ago. But we had a lot in between. And we sold our business, '78. We bought it back in '88. We sold it back in '92. But at this moment, as I'm talking to you, I'm retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even during the years that you had sold the business, you didn't own it, but you still ran it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Ran it for the 10 years straight with it. Every time we ran it. We ran it all the time. We sold it and we ran it together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the company was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The original company that bought us was [? Claude ?] Peabody. The [INAUDIBLE], as you probably know about. But today they're nonexistent, they are-- because then somebody else bought them out. It was a-- it's a real--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did-- what-- when you and your friend opened the business, what did you call it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dobie Industries, after Dobie Gillis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you-- what did you produce?

speaker: interviewee
text: We manufactured children's sportswear. All-- all domestic. none-- no-- no imports, till we sold it a second time. We did all the mass-- we had all our factories in North Carolina. And we'd employ a lot. We employed-- but sometimes about 600 people. We employed a lot of people. We did a lot of volume. And then we sold it. And we bought it back. Because the company became so big, [? Claude ?] Peabody was bought out by JP-- by-- by another company, which was [INAUDIBLE] And then became-- they became, like, a $2.5-billion company. And then another guy bought them. The Fruit of the Loom company bought them. So it became so big. So they wanted to get rid of this company that was only $50 million. And we employed a lot of people. We really didn't buy-- so we bought it back to keep everybody working. And we kept it for the next-- '88-- for the next five years. And then we sold it to an investment company. Which now really is-- they merged with somebody else. And the Dobie name is still around. Dobie Industries is still in the business. But we don't own it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been retired since March?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since the end of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now tell me about some of the other things you do not related to work since retirement and before retirement. Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since retirement, really, that's very-- too sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just your activities--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very active--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your-- your-- your community activities.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was active. You know, I-- Lifeline, which is an organization, supports a lot of children. You know, they-- they don't-- instead of sending them to some institution, they support them. And it's a very large institution in New York City right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are these children with physical handicaps?

speaker: interviewee
text: All--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or mental handicaps?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- all physical and mental handicap. If not for this organization, they'd all be in-- they'd-- they'd all be in-- in institutions. And I'm-- a [? UGA. ?] And I'm a big contribute-- contributor over there. And I do a lot of work for HIAS. I also belong to a club for the last 30 years, which I was, at one point, I was very active. At one point I was President of the club.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a country club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Country club, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A golf club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Old Westbury Golf and Country Club. And I'm still very active there. I also have a home in Florida. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you belong to a synagogue here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belong to Beth Sholom on Roslyn Road. I belonged to the synagogue since I moved into this house in 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has the community changed in the-- the 30-something years since you're here?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's gotten bigger. Our temple is very large today. It probably has almost 1,200 families. It's one of the very progressive. It's Conservative. We have the-- this-- our rabbi was Rabbi Sternstein, which is probably a very known man in-- in the circles of synagogues and in Israel. He just retired recently.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how is it that you didn't have a bar mitzvah at that synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- initially-- this is a Conservative synagogue. They don't-- they-- they don't read haftarah on Friday night. And then my granddaughter belongs to Temple Torah on-- in Dix Hills. I did not want to take away from her. And I wanted to be together with her. So I couldn't bring her to my temple. So I guess I went to her temple. And Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], which is also very known, and he was a very, very fine rabbi. He let me do a lot of things which our temple would not let me do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, first of all, they did mine on Friday night. We-- we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say a Torah blessing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to say-- say a Torah blessing. Then read-- then read the Torah. And at that-- I only invited, really, most of the people that knew me, not so much that knew my granddaughter, from my side. He also let me lead the congregation. I brought my cousin from Israel. They let me lead the congregation during the last Kaddish. So I said Kaddish for the-- for all my-- my aunts and cousins, which are-- even he-- he was very-- didn't want to do it. But I talked him into it. What now, I talked-- And I-- he did a very great job for me. And certain things, that what he did, I think my temple wouldn't do. Well, because they're Conservative. This is a Reform temple. And Rabbi [INAUDIBLE] was very nice. And I wanted to be with my granddaughter. So it did not interfere with my granddaughter's bat mitzvah, you know. So everything that was done was on a Friday night. And this was done two years ago. And now I'm a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. I think we're going to stop the tape now. Do we stop the tape to bring in the-- to do the stills? [SIGHS] We're continuing with the tape four before we move onto the pictures and introducing the family, with one final question for Mr. Peters. It's now 50 years since your war experiences. Is there a particular message that you would want to leave for your grandchildren and all the other children from what you lived through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I would like to leave them with a message. Don't forget your roots, where you come from. And make sure the world doesn't forget what happened. And it's up to you to-- to remind them. And it's up to your generation and generations after you. Make sure that it never happens again to Jewish people or any other ethnic group. Because if you forget it, history repeats itself. Maybe not in my lifetime or your lifetime, but it repeats itself. So you-- your generation has to make sure the world does not forget about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Who is in this picture and when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture right after the war, that we all survived, my family. And from right the left is myself, my sister, and my brother. Below from the right-hand side, is my aunt. On the left is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say everybody's name as you go through it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my-- from my-- on the bottom, from my right, it's [? Chalaya. ?] Sarah, Sarah, my mother. Aaron, Fanny, and myself, Menachem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture-- this picture was taken in 1945, right after the war in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in-- in-- it was 1946. This is my Hebrew class in the-- the DP camp. I'm in the-- I'm on-- in the middle on top. And this is the only time where I finally felt normal as a kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this photograph was the end of 1945. And from the right is my cousin, Aaron [INAUDIBLE], and from the left is his friend, Ezra Sherman. And

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was taken in Bytom, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's in these-- this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture had to be taken before the war, in 1938. On the right-hand side is my cousin, Esther, what did not survive. And my last-- on my left-hand side is my cousin, [? Ronit, ?] who-- who did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was taken in Austria. That's around 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you happen to have the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't answer that. I wish I could. It's-- it's-- it's the only one I have-- the only one that I have, a prewar picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cant think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say-- say that you don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say-- do you know their last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the moment I can't think of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to put a mic--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Am I ready? This-- this picture was taken in 1954, in Camp Kilmer, before I went-- before they send me back to Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- were you in the US Army at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was in US Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a cadet uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this picture, I was a private. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about this picture. When was it taken and where? And who's in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- this picture was taken in-- in 1993. from-- from right to left, that's my son-in-law, Larry, my daughter, Linda, my son, Mark, myself, my wife, Shirley, my grandson, Scott, and my granddaughter, Melissa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion for the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the-- the second marriage of my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taking place?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken at Old Westbury Golf and Country Club.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? I was honored by Lifeline, which is an organization for retarded children in which-- in which they take care and send them to school. Otherwise they would all have to be institutionalized. And most of the kids, after a few years, go back to normal and go to normal schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please explain this plaque.

speaker: interviewee
text: This plaque was given to me by [? Gulotta, ?] who's the executive-- was their executive-- from--

speaker: interviewee
text: County.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Nassau County, for being an active participant in CP and soliciting a lot-- and-- and doing all the solicitations for them over the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: CP is?

speaker: interviewer
text: CP is Cerebral Palsy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cerebral Palsy, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this plaque?

speaker: interviewee
text: This plaque was given to me by Old Westbury Golf and Country Club for being a Board Member and also as President for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been a member of the country club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since 1965, 30 years. And I've been active there all-- probably the last 15 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Peters, would you introduce your family, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Shirley. In the back row, my son-- my son, Mark, my grandson Scott, my daughter, Linda, and my granddaughter, Melissa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anyone like to say anything at this time? MARK (SON): Yes, I'd like to say one thing. I don't know about other families, but as we were growing up, we were definitely very sheltered from this. And I don't-- I don't believe that I really ever have-- have ever heard the story that my father has told. And I am genuinely interested in hearing that and the stories of others. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--
